title: Once-for-All: Scalable Simultaneous Forecasting via Equilibrium State Estimation
abstract: We introduce Equilibrium State Estimation (ESE), a novel paradigm for simultaneous prediction, where multiple interacting systems require separate yet coordinated forecasts. Such scenarios often arise in real-world such as economics and healthcare modeling.  Unlike existing approaches that predict one system at a time, ESE forecasts all systems in a single pass.  It first estimates the equilibrium state across systems, then generates holistic forecasts based on the difference between the current state and the estimated equilibrium.  Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets, including currency exchange and COVID-19 spread modeling, demonstrate that ESE is at least as accurate as state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods while being significantly faster.  In addition, ESE integrates seamlessly with conventional predictors, combining their accuracy with its exceptional efficiency and delivering a 10–70× speedup. With linear-time complexity, ESE scales far better than SOTA methods as the number of systems increases. Moreover, it remains accurate under diverse perturbations, establishing ESE as a fast, generalizable, robust, and scalable multi-prediction method.
decision_comment:
The paper proposes equilibrium State Estimation (ESE) to concurrently forecast multiple interacting systems in a hierarchical way: first, forecast an aggregate quantity and then redistribute it across individual systems using an estimated equilibrium state. Reviewers found the paper practically motivated and recognized several clear strengths, such as computational complexity advantages, scalability, and the flexibility of using ESE as a lightweight procedure operating on top of existing forecasting models. The empirical study is extensive enough and covers synthetic and real datasets, multiple horizons, and a diverse set of baselines, and the reported efficiency gains are substantial.

Authors’ and reviewers’ engagement in the rebuttal has been helpful in clarifying several important points (e.g., the notion of equilibrium) and in providing evidence of robustness to practical impediments. Nevertheless, some concerns do remain. In particular, the relationship to top-down or hierarchical forecasting could be positioned more explicitly, and the paper would benefit from a more careful discussion of when aggregation may or may not lose subsystem-specific dynamics in highly heterogeneous settings. I also agree with the reviewers that some claims and framing should be moderated so that the presentation more closely matches the evidence currently provided. 

Overall, I view this paper favorably and recommend a “weak accept”, following the recommendation of the reviewers after they have re-evaluated all the strengths and weaknesses of the paper and adjusted their ratings accordingly.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces Equilibrium State Estimation, or ESE, as a new framework for simultaneous forecasting over multiple interacting systems. Instead of predicting each system one by one, the method first estimates a shared equilibrium state across the whole ensemble and then forecasts future values from the gap between the current state and that estimated equilibrium. The paper argues that this is a better fit for settings like exchange rates or regional COVID spread, where systems clearly affect each other, and the experiments on synthetic data as well as real-world exchange-rate and epidemic datasets suggest that ESE is generally competitive with strong forecasting baselines while being much faster. I think the main message comes through pretty clearly, and the idea itself is easy to follow even though the setup is a bit unusual at first.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses: One thing I liked a lot is that the paper does not present ESE only as a standalone model, but also as a module that can be combined with many existing predictors. That makes the contribution feel more practical, not just conceptually new. Across the reported exchange-rate and COVID-19 experiments, ESE often improves or at least preserves predictive accuracy while reducing runtime very substantially, and in the large COVID setting the reported speedups can even exceed 70 times for some baselines. The scalability story is also fairly convincing, because the paper connects the method design to linear-time complexity and then backs this up with experiments across different numbers of systems, horizons, and input lengths. Overall, the paper has a nice systems-level perspective, and it does seem to offer a genuinely useful way to forecast many coupled targets at once.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
I also had a more theory-oriented question about the notion of equilibrium used in the paper. The framing through Nash equilibrium is interesting and, honestly, one of the parts that made the paper stand out to me at first, since the manuscript motivates ESE by analyzing interactions among internal systems and even writes the equilibrium condition through a utility-style formulation over attributes. 

At the same time, the actual estimation procedure is driven by so called "cointegration", and the paper later clarifies that the equilibrium in ESE should be understood as a statistical equilibrium rather than a game-theoretic one. It would therefore be very helpful if the authors could sharpen this distinction a bit more formally. What exactly is inherited from the Nash-equilibrium analogy, and what is only heuristic motivation? In particular, is the estimated equilibrium state meant to approximate some Nash-like fixed point of interacting systems, or is it better viewed as a long-run statistical compatibility condition with no strategic optimality semantics? 

I would also be curious whether the authors see any principled relationship, or an explicit separation, between their notion of equilibrium and other classical concepts such as Pareto-efficient states. For instance, could an ESE equilibrium ever be interpreted as efficient at the system level without being stable in a game-theoretic sense, or are these ideas completely orthogonal in this framework? I think clarifying this theoretical positioning would make the paper even stronger, because the intuition is compelling, but right now the boundary between the Nash-inspired setup and the cointegration-based estimator felt a little blurry to me.
reviews.limitations: No particular limitations were identified in terms of the overall framework. The paper does provide a definition of the Nash equilibrium concept that it intends to use. However, the theoretical characterization remains somewhat incomplete. In particular, the manuscript does not discuss aspects such as convergence guarantees, reachability of the equilibrium under the proposed dynamics, or how the formulation relates to different game-theoretic regimes (e.g., non-zero-sum, cooperative, or non-cooperative settings). A clearer treatment of these theoretical aspects would substantially strengthen the work. For this reason, my overall assessment remains at the level of a weak accept.
reviews.final_justification: As the authors fully address my questions with constructive feedback, I will maintain the original score.

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Equilibrium State Estimation (ESE), a scalable framework designed to forecast multiple interacting systems simultaneously. Rather than directly modeling the complex multivariate dynamics of all target variables, ESE leverages exogenous attribute data to compute a statistical equilibrium: representing a steady-state proportional distribution of the constituent systems relative to the whole. An iterative cointegration test ensures the estimated distribution reflects a long-run stable relationship. To generate temporal forecasts, ESE isolates the global trend by calculating the aggregate sum of all systems. An external base model is then used to forecast this single aggregate sum (effectively acting as a univariate forecaster), and ESE subsequently disaggregates this global forecast into system-specific predictions based on the computed equilibrium weights. Empirical evaluations across synthetic, macroeconomic, and epidemiological datasets demonstrate that ESE provides a computationally lightweight, top-down alternative to standard multivariate forecasting.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
# Strengths: 
- The authors provide a robust computational complexity analysis, confirming that ESE achieves linear scalability. The empirical evaluation spans 13 baselines across multiple datasets, input lengths, and horizons.

- The computational pipeline is well-illustrated, and the appendix contains a wealth of supplementary details and convergence plots.

- The use of cointegration testing as a dynamic stopping criterion for learning proportional weights from static metadata is a creative bridge between classical econometrics and machine learning inference.

# Weaknesses: 
- Violation of Channel Independence (CI). The paper evaluates ESE as a wrapper around models like PatchTST and DLinear. These models achieve state-of-the-art performance explicitly through Channel Independence, treating each variate independently to avoid cross-channel noise contamination and manage subsystem heterogeneity. However, ESE (Eq. 8) forces these models to forecast a single, global aggregate sum ($\mathcal{MS}_t$). This completely destroys the CI inductive bias of the base models, reducing them to simple univariate forecasters on a mixed-signal aggregate.

- Channel Mixing and Heterogeneity. It is well-known in time series literature that mixing heterogeneous channels often worsens forecasting performance due to the addition of localized noise and the destructive interference of out-of-phase signals (e.g., regional COVID-19 peaks occurring weeks apart). By summing all subsystems into a single scalar, ESE mathematically obliterates high-frequency, subsystem-specific temporal dynamics. The paper fails to theoretically justify why discarding CI and reverting to global channel mixing is sound.

- Dynamical Systems and Ergodicity. The paper relies heavily on the concept of an interacting "equilibrium state." From a dynamical systems perspective, if the coupled multi-system ensemble is ergodic and evolving on an equilibrium manifold, Takens' Embedding Theorem dictates that the full state space of the system can be reconstructed from a sufficiently long observation of a single subsystem. The authors do not reconcile their explicit, high-dimensional attribute modeling with this fundamental property, leaving the theoretical necessity of their approach mathematically ungrounded.

- The theoretical framing is deeply disjointed. The motivation relies heavily on Game Theory and Nash Equilibrium (Section 3.1, Appendix B/C). However, the systems modeled (COVID-19 regions, fiat currencies) are not rational utility-maximizing agents. The actual implementation is purely statistical (OLS, MLE, cointegration). This forced game-theoretic metaphor acts as a distraction from the underlying statistical mechanics.


- The paper claims SOTA multivariate models struggle to utilize attribute data effectively (e.g., Table 5). However, it is a well-documented phenomenon that in deep forecasting models (especially Transformers) strong target autocorrelation starves exogenous covariates of gradient signals. ESE bypasses this entirely by decoupling the attributes from the neural network and processing them through a simplistic linear OLS/MLE layer. The empirical gains of ESE likely do not stem from a superior architectural paradigm, but rather from the baselines' failure to properly optimize against the gradient starvation, which ESE circumvents via a severe linear bottleneck.


- Equivalence to Hierarchical Forecasting. Mechanistically, ESE operates like a Top-Down Hierarchical Time Series forecasting method. It forecasts a top-level aggregate ($\mathcal{MS}_{t+h}$) and disaggregates it based on estimated proportions ($\gamma_i$). The paper fails to position its contribution relative to the extensive literature on optimal reconciliation and cross-sectional hierarchical forecasting, nor does it compare ESE against standard top-down HTS baselines.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Ergodicity and Takens' Theorem: If the multi-system ensemble represents an ergodic dynamical system moving towards an equilibrium state, Takens' Embedding Theorem implies that the phase space of the entire system can be reconstructed from a long-enough observation of a single subsystem. Given this, what is the formal mathematical justification for requiring the explicit top-down modeling of all subsystems and their attributes, rather than relying on temporal embeddings? Explanation: Reconciling the method with established dynamical systems theory will heavily influence the evaluation of the paper's theoretical Soundness.

Channel Independence vs. Channel Mixing: Architectures like PatchTST and DLinear derive their SOTA performance from Channel Independence (CI), which avoids the noise, variance, and feature starvation associated with mixing heterogeneous channels. ESE forces a channel-mixing approach by summing all systems into a single aggregate $\mathcal{MS}_t$ before forecasting. How do you reconcile ESE's mechanics with established literature showing that mixing channels degrades performance, and doesn't this entirely strip the base models of their primary architectural advantage? Explanation: This addresses whether the observed performance gains are a true architectural breakthrough or a mechanical artifact of reducing a multivariate task to a univariate one, impacting Originality and Soundness.

Handling Heterogeneous Shocks: By aggregating all systems into a global scalar, localized temporal dynamics are lost. How does ESE mathematically or empirically prevent transient, localized shocks (e.g., a sudden, isolated COVID-19 outbreak in one region) from corrupting the aggregate global forecast, which is then erroneously smeared across all other stable regions via the proportional weights? Explanation: Resolving this will clarify the robustness of the global aggregation step under highly heterogeneous real-world conditions.

Driver Channel Blindness and Fair Baselines: In deep multivariate models, driver channel blindness is a known issue where strong autocorrelations prevent the model from learning effectively from exogenous attributes. ESE bypasses this by using an explicit OLS/MLE step outside the network. In your experiments (e.g., Table 5), did the multivariate baselines simply fail to utilize attributes because of driver blindness? Does ESE's linear decoupling prevent the discovery of complex non-linear spatio-temporal interactions? Explanation: A strong defense of the linear attribute combination strategy versus end-to-end deep representation learning will significantly impact the evaluation of the method's Significance.

Top-Down Hierarchical Forecasting: Structurally, ESE behaves exactly like a top-down hierarchical forecasting method: it forecasts the aggregate $\mathcal{MS}_{t+h}$ and disaggregates it based on estimated proportions. How does ESE compare theoretically and empirically to standard top-down hierarchical forecasting methods (e.g., historical proportions, ARIMA-based reconciliation), and why were no HTS baselines included? Explanation: Contextualizing the novelty against established hierarchical literature is critical for accurately evaluating Originality.
reviews.limitations:
The authors explicitly dismiss the discussion of potential negative societal impacts in their Impact Statement, stating: "There are many potential societal consequences of our work, none of which we feel must be specifically highlighted here."

This is an inadequate and dismissive response for a top-tier machine learning venue. Given that the method is explicitly designed for, and evaluated on, epidemiology (COVID-19 spread) and macroeconomics (currency exchange), the authors must be far more critical of their own structural assumptions. Constructive additions must include:

Acknowledging the severe risks of model miscalibration and resource misallocation if the "equilibrium" assumption strictly fails during sudden macroeconomic shocks or localized epidemiological outbreaks.

Discussing how enforcing a global aggregate trend onto individual systems via slow-moving proportions might chronically under-predict tail-events in smaller subsystems, potentially masking critical, localized anomalies and delaying targeted public health interventions.

Addressing the risk of bias introduced by the exogenous attributes (e.g., using skewed, lagging, or historically prejudiced demographic data to dictate regional healthcare allocations).

reviews.summary: This paper targets a less-explored task: the joint forecasting of multiple coupled subsystem sequences within a supersystem. It proposes using an Equilibrium State to estimate the overall equilibrium tendency of the system and adjust the model’s predictions based on this tendency. The method first estimates the influence of various attributes on the equilibrium, and obtains the proportional distribution of different subsystems by estimating the equilibrium state. Afterwards, predictions for the subsystems can be obtained simply by forecasting the future global state of the supersystem and then distributing it to the subsystems, which ensures high efficiency by decoupling the number of subsystems from the number of future states the model needs to predict.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Soundness
The paper starts directly with theoretical introduction, lacking elaboration on the task itself and prior work for this task. The method is explained and verified clearly and intuitively, and the experiments support the claimed performance. Structurally, the paper is largely complete, but several issues are raised below, each requiring a response:
1. The paper lacks sufficient related works. Since the authors do not claim to propose the task of Simultaneous Forecasting but focus more on equilibrium states, the historical background of this task should be clarified.
2. The method appears to assume that the supersystem is closed, but in practice (e.g., COVID-19 data), the system is open. How should this be interpreted?
3. Are there any limits to the scope for constructing such system relationships? For instance, if all regions worldwide are treated as subsystems and the globe as the supersystem to predict the temperature of each subsystem, what level of spatial granularity can be supported?
4. The method uses multi-step observations to forecast a 1-step horizon, which deviates from the standard setup in time series forecasting. Is it meaningful to compare time series forecasting models under this setting?
5. Phase differences naturally exist between subsystems. Although this forecasting framework simplifies supersystem prediction, it shifts the modeling burden to subsystem relationships—such as phase differences, which may not be reflected in 1-step forecasting. Can performance be maintained for longer prediction horizons or autoregressive forecasting?
6. Why does adding ESE guarantee the original model’s performance as a lower bound? The authors only show results without analysis.

Presentation
The writing is relatively fluent, and the explanation of the Equilibrium State is appropriate. However, the description of the task itself is insufficient. Furthermore, only experimental settings and results are presented, with inadequate analysis of the experiments.

Significance
Due to the insufficient explanation of the task and previous works, I am currently unable to judge its significance.

Originality
The way the paper employs the Equilibrium State is intuitive and simply implemented. Despite the insufficient task description, I believe the method still shows a certain degree of novelty.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: All the questions have been raised above. Please reply to them in order of priority (by index) from top to bottom as much as possible. Thank you very much. I temporarily lower the score. I believe the authors' design is reasonable, and I hope they can supplement more experiments to enable me to more fully evaluate the model's performance and the support for the proposed views. During this process, I hope the authors will have the opportunity to consider deeper insights. At that time, I will raise the score based on the authors' response.
reviews.final_justification: We appreciate the authors' response. The issues have been largely resolved, and the applicability of the method has been further verified. However, the authors need to revise some expressions in the manuscript. We will raise the score to 4.
